rkagi 0.1.0

    • rkagi Quickstart Guide

rkagi Quickstart Guide

Rainer Krug

rkagi: R Client for Kagi APIs

Installation

Installation is at the moment only via github:

if (requireNamespace("remotes", quietly = TRUE) == FALSE) {
  install.packages("remotes")
}
remotes::install_github("rkrug/rkagi")

Getting a Kagi API key

To use rkagi you need an active Kagi account and an API subscription.

  1. Kagi account

    • Sign up at https://kagi.com.
    • The API is only available for paid Kagi accounts (Starter, Standard, or Professional).
    • You need to enable billing; free trial accounts cannot use the API.

  2. Enable API access

    • Go to your Kagi Account Dashboard.
    • Under API Access, generate a new API key.
    • Copy the key to a safe place.

  3. Store the key securely
    Use the keyring package to save your key in the system keychain:

    keyring::key_set("API_kagi")

    You will be prompted once to enter your key. It will then be stored securely in the system keychain.

  4. Use the key in rkagi
    When creating a connection, resolve the key lazily:

    conn <- new_kagi_connection(
  endpoint = "search",
  api_key = function() keyring::key_get("API_kagi")
)

    The key is only looked up when a request is performed, never stored in plain text.

  5. Respect limits and billing
    Each endpoint (Search, Enrich, Summarizer) has its own pricing and rate limits.
    API usage deducts from your Kagi balance. See https://help.kagi.com/kagi/api/ for details.

Package concepts

  • Connections
    Represent the link to the API. Created with new_kagi_connection(base_url, endpoint, api_key).
    Usually constructed automatically by the higher-level *_once() helpers.

  • Requests
    Lightweight S3 objects that describe what you want to send to the API
    (e.g. kagi_search, kagi_enrich, kagi_summarize).

  • Results
    Typed S3 objects that wrap the API response, including the raw JSON, metadata, and a parsed tibble or summary
    (e.g. kagi_search_results, kagi_enrich_results, kagi_summarize_results).

  • Helpers
    Functions to make life easier, such as build_kagi_query() to compose advanced search queries, or open_kagi_query() to preview queries in a browser.

Build a precise query

rkagi provides helpers to compose complex search queries safely.
You can combine free-text terms with structured operators:

  • filetype: – restrict by file extension (vector values combined with OR)
  • site: – restrict by domain (vector values combined with OR)
  • inurl: – require substrings in the URL (values combined with AND)
  • intitle: – require words in the page title (values combined with AND)
library(rkagi)

q <- build_kagi_query(
  query    = 'biodiversity "annual report"',
  filetype = c("pdf", "docx"),
  site     = c("example.com", "gov"),
  inurl    = c("2024", "report"),
  intitle  = "summary"
)

q
#> biodiversity "annual report" (filetype:pdf OR filetype:docx) (site:example.com OR site:gov) inurl:2024 inurl:report intitle:summary

# Preview directly in your browser
open_kagi_query(q)

Search API (closed beta)

Create a connection and a prepared search, then perform it. Results are typed S3 objects that print nicely and provide helpers to extract hits, related queries, and metadata.

conn <- new_kagi_connection(endpoint = "search")  # api_key resolved lazily

s <- new_kagi_search(conn, q = q, limit = 5)
s                                # prints the request

res <- kagi_perform(s, path = tempfile(fileext = ".json"))  # optional: save raw JSON
res                               # prints summary (hits, related, timing)

hits    <- kagi_hits(res)         # tibble of t == 0 results
related <- kagi_related(res)      # character vector of t == 1 related queries
meta    <- kagi_meta(res)         # list of { id, node, ms, api_balance }

One-liner 

res <- kagi_search_once("openalex api", limit = 3)
kagi_hits(res)

Result structure

A kagi_search_results object contains:

  • json: raw JSON string
  • meta: list with response metadata (e.g. latency, node, API balance)
  • data: tibble of search results (or NULL if empty)

Use the extractor helpers (kagi_hits(), kagi_related(), kagi_meta()) rather than accessing the slots directly.

Enrich API

The Enrich endpoint returns higher-signal results from specialized indices. The workflow mirrors Search: request → perform → helpers.

conn_e <- new_kagi_connection(endpoint = "enrich")  # or 'enrich/web' depending on API flavor
req_e  <- new_kagi_enrich(conn_e, q = q, limit = 5)
res_e  <- kagi_perform(req_e, path = tempfile(fileext = ".json"))

res_e                          # print summary
enrich_items <- enrich_extract(res_e)  # tibble of enrichment hits

One-liner 

res_e <- kagi_enrich_once("open data", limit = 5)
enrich_extract(res_e)

Result structure

A kagi_enrich_results object contains:

  • json: raw JSON string
  • meta: list with response metadata (e.g. latency, node, API balance)
  • data: tibble of enrichment hits (or NULL if empty)

Use the extractor helper enrich_extract() rather than accessing the slots directly.

Universal Summarizer

The Universal Summarizer can condense either a URL (GET) or raw text (POST).
Results are returned as S3 objects containing summary, tokens, and meta.

conn_s <- new_kagi_connection(endpoint = "summarize")

# --- URL mode ---
req_s <- new_summarize_request(
  conn_s,
  url             = "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZSRHeXYDLko",
  engine          = "muriel",
  summary_type    = "summary",
  target_language = "EN"
)

res_s <- summarize_perform(req_s, path = tempfile(fileext = ".json"))

summarizer_output(res_s)  # summary text
summarizer_tokens(res_s)  # token usage
summarizer_meta(res_s)    # metadata
# --- Text mode ---
req_s2 <- new_summarize_request(
  conn_s,
  text            = "Paste long text here ...",
  engine          = "cecil",
  summary_type    = "takeaway",
  target_language = "EN",
  cache           = FALSE
)

res_s2 <- summarize_perform(req_s2)
summarizer_output(res_s2)

One-liner 

res <- kagi_summarize_once(
  url = "https://www.example.com/long-article",
  engine = "muriel",
  summary_type = "summary",
  target_language = "EN"
)
summarizer_output(res)

Result structure

A kagi_summarize_results object contains:

  • summary: summarizer output text
  • tokens: token usage reported by the API
  • meta: list of response metadata (latency, node, API balance)
  • raw_json: the raw JSON string (and written to disk if path was provided)

Error Handling

HTTP errors are converted to informative R errors using httr2::req_error(). API-provided messages (if present) are preserved in thrown conditions. Consider defining custom condition classes to allow tryCatch() by users.

Rate Limits & Courtesy

Respect Retry-After headers and consider a simple backoff on 429/503 responses. Avoid hammering endpoints; cache raw JSON on disk when feasible by using the path argument of *_perform().

Session info 

sessionInfo()

On this page
